_comment: Jurisdiction configuration for the Election Administrator Trainer, filled in for the Commonwealth of Virginia (Title 24.2). Change 'county' to your locality. State authorities cite the Code of Virginia with official law.lis.virginia.gov links; the Election Law Navigator (https://electionlawnavigator.org/) remains the research link. For a blank, any-state template see examples/jurisdiction.generic.json. After editing, run `node build-config.js` (file://) or serve over HTTP. Training simulation — not legal advice.

jurisdiction.office: Office of the General Registrar & Director of Elections

resources.navigatorNote: Election Law Program (National Center for State Courts & William & Mary Law School) — 30,000+ statutes, regulations, and advisory opinions across all 50 states, organized by 100+ topic tags.

vbm.ballotDeadline: Received by 7:00 PM on Election Day (close of polls), or postmarked on or before Election Day and received by the statutory deadline (§ 24.2-709)
vbm.cureDeadline: the cure deadline set by Va. Code § 24.2-709.1

vbm_cure.navigatorTopic: Absentee / Mail-In Voting — Signature Verification & Cure

authorities.note: Rejecting a mail ballot for an envelope-statement defect without notice and an opportunity to cure raises due-process concerns.

If a returned ballot's voter affirmation is incomplete or a § 24.2-707 procedure was not followed in a way that would void the ballot, the general registrar must notify the voter and provide instructions to correct it so the ballot can count.

vbm_deadline.navigatorTopic: Absentee / Mail-In Voting — Receipt Deadlines & Postmark Rules

authorities.note: Requires transmission of ballots to military and overseas voters ≥ 45 days before a federal election.
Sets the deadline for a mail ballot to be received (and any postmark allowance). Confirm the current rule, which has changed across recent sessions.
Guarantees the right to cast a provisional ballot and requires it be counted if the voter is determined eligible under state law.

authorities.cite: Va. Code § 24.2-653 (provisional ballots); § 24.2-653.01 (electoral board determines validity)
authorities.note: A voter lacking required ID, or whose registration is in question, votes provisionally; the electoral board meets after the election to determine whether each provisional voter was entitled to vote in the precinct.

authorities.cite: Anderson–Burdick framework (U.S. Const. amends. I, XIV)
authorities.note: Ballot-access burdens are weighed against the state's regulatory interests; requirements must not be unduly burdensome.

Candidates for a county/city governing body or elected school board need 125 valid signatures of qualified voters (50 in an election district of 1,000 or fewer registered voters); each petition page must carry the witness affidavit. Filing deadlines are in § 24.2-507.

authorities.cite: Va. Const. art. II, § 5 & Va. Code § 24.2-500 et seq. (qualifications to hold office)
authorities.note: A candidate must be a qualified voter and meet the residency/age/office requirements; constitutional ineligibility is not curable.
Federal periodic disclosure (federal candidates/committees only; Virginia state & local committees are governed by the Code of Virginia).

authorities.cite: Va. Code Title 24.2, Ch. 9.3 — Campaign Finance Disclosure Act of 2006; § 24.2-953.2 (late filing)
authorities.note: Late filing of a required report carries a civil penalty not to exceed $500; a second or subsequent violation in an election cycle is $1,000. The penalty is set by statute — flat, not a per-day accrual.
Prohibits disability discrimination by public entities; voting must be accessible. See DOJ ADA Checklist for Polling Places.

authorities.cite: Voting Accessibility for the Elderly and Handicapped Act, 52 U.S.C. § 20102
authorities.note: Requires accessible polling places for federal elections, or an accessible alternative means of voting.

Polling places must be accessible per the Virginians with Disabilities Act (§ 51.5-1 et seq.), VAEHA, and the ADA; if a place becomes unusable or inaccessible, an accessible alternative must be provided with notice and State Board approval.
Requires bilingual election materials in covered jurisdictions (some Virginia localities are covered).
A voter who needs assistance may be helped by a person of their choice.
Voting-system standards: error-rate limits, audit capacity, and a permanent paper record — the basis for logic & accuracy testing.

authorities.cite: Va. Code § 24.2-625 et seq. (voting systems; approval, testing & sealing)
authorities.note: Voting equipment must be certified and tested for accuracy before use; ballot form requirements are in § 24.2-613.

List maintenance must be uniform and nondiscriminatory; a registrant may NOT be removed solely for failing to vote, and address-based removal requires a forwardable confirmation notice plus a waiting period of {regWait}.
Statewide computerized voter-registration database and list-maintenance coordination.
The Department of Elections and general registrars maintain the rolls and send address-confirmation notices; specific removal grounds are in § 24.2-427.
Permits removal on the voter's request, death, a disqualifying criminal conviction, mental incapacity (as state law provides), or a confirmed change of residence — and only on those grounds.

The general registrar shall cancel the registration of persons known to be deceased, disqualified by felony conviction or adjudicated incapacity, non-citizens, or who have moved from the Commonwealth — or upon the voter's signed request.
Federal-election records (including the materials behind a canvass) must be retained for 22 months.

authorities.cite: Va. Code § 24.2-671 (electoral board ascertains results); § 24.2-675 (abstract of votes)
authorities.note: Each electoral board meets at or before 5:00 PM the day after the election to open returns, reconcile, and complete the abstract of votes; the certified result is conclusive except as challenged under Chapter 8.
